mnemonics.title: CLASSIFY BMW Types
mnemonics.content: I P S P C C R G - Infectious, Pathological, Sharps, Pharmaceutical, Cytotoxic, Chemical, Radioactive, General

mnemonics.title: BMW Sources Memory
mnemonics.content: H C L R A F P H - Hospitals, Clinics, Laboratories, Research, Animal houses, Funeral homes, Pharmacies, Blood banks

mnemonics.title: Colour Coding - RIP BWR
mnemonics.content: R-Red (Infectious), I-Yellow (Human Anatomical), P-White (Sharps), B-Blue (Glassware/Chemical), W-Blue (Microbiology)

acronyms.usage: Any waste from healthcare facilities that may contain infectious or hazardous materials
Global body providing BMW management guidelines
Comprehensive system for handling, treating, and disposing of BMW
Drugs that destroy cells, used in chemotherapy, require special disposal

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hospital floor plan with colour-coded bins: RED for contaminated bandages, YELLOW for tissues, WHITE puncture-proof containers for needles, BLUE for glass bottles - each area has its designated bin
Think of a pie chart where 75-90% is 'safe' grey area (general waste like paper, food), and only 10-25% is 'danger zone' (red zone with pathogens, chemicals, sharps)
Draw an arrow from NEEDLE (Sharps) → PUNCTURE SKIN → BLOOD STREAM → DISEASE (HIV/Hepatitis) - emphasizing why sharps are most dangerous

analogies.analogy: Just as a kitchen has both food waste (biodegradable) and broken glass (hazardous), a hospital has general waste (paper, food) and hazardous BMW (needles, cultures)

Infectious waste is like a germ-loaded sponge you clean with; pathological waste is like a severed organ - both need special handling but for different reasons (infectious vs recognizable human tissue)
Think of cytotoxic drugs as 'precision bombs' that target cancer cells - they work by causing DNA damage, so the waste also has DNA-damaging potential and must be handled carefully
